lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So much for this, sir: now shall you see the other;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You do remember all the circumstance?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Remember it, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, in my heart there was a kind of fighting,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That would not let me sleep: methought I lay

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Worse than the mutines in the bilboes. Rashly,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And praised be rashness for it, let us know,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Our indiscretion sometimes serves us well,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: When our deep plots do pall: and that should teach us

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: There’s a divinity that shapes our ends,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Rough-hew them how we will—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: That is most certain.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Up from my cabin,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My sea-gown scarf’d about me, in the dark

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Groped I to find out them; had my desire,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Finger’d their packet, and in fine withdrew

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To mine own room again; making so bold,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My fears forgetting manners, to unseal

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Their grand commission; where I found, Horatio—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O royal knavery!—an exact command,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Larded with many several sorts of reasons

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Importing Denmark’s health and England’s too,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With, ho! such bugs and goblins in my life,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That, on the supervise, no leisure bated,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, not to stay the grinding of the axe,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My head should be struck off.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Is’t possible?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Here’s the commission: read it at more leisure.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But wilt thou hear me how I did proceed?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I beseech you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Being thus be-netted round with villainies—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ere I could make a prologue to my brains,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: They had begun the play—I sat me down,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Devised a new commission, wrote it fair:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I once did hold it, as our statists do,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A baseness to write fair and labour’d much

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How to forget that learning, but, sir, now

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It did me yeoman’s service: wilt thou know

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The effect of what I wrote?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Ay, good my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: An earnest conjuration from the king,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As England was his faithful tributary,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As love between them like the palm might flourish,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As peace should still her wheaten garland wear

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And stand a comma ’tween their amities,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And many such-like “As”es of great charge,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That, on the view and knowing of these contents,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Without debatement further, more or less,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He should the bearers put to sudden death,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Not shriving-time allow’d.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: How was this seal’d?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, even in that was heaven ordinant.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I had my father’s signet in my purse,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Which was the model of that Danish seal;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Folded the writ up in form of the other,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Subscribed it, gave’t the impression, placed it safely,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The changeling never known. Now, the next day

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Was our sea-fight; and what to this was sequent

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thou know’st already.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: So Guildenstern and Rosencrantz go to’t.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, man, they did make love to this employment;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: They are not near my conscience; their defeat

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Does by their own insinuation grow:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis dangerous when the baser nature comes

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Between the pass and fell incensed points

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Of mighty opposites.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Why, what a king is this!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Does it not, think’st thee, stand me now upon—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He that hath kill’d my king and whored my mother,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Popp’d in between the election and my hopes,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thrown out his angle for my proper life,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And with such cozenage—is’t not perfect conscience,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To quit him with this arm? and is’t not to be damn’d,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To let this canker of our nature come

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In further evil?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It must be shortly known to him from England

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What is the issue of the business there.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It will be short: the interim is mine;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And a man’s life’s no more than to say “One.”

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But I am very sorry, good Horatio,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That to Laertes I forgot myself;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For, by the image of my cause, I see

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The portraiture of his: I’ll court his favours:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But, sure, the bravery of his grief did put me

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Into a towering passion.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Peace! who comes here?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Your lordship is right welcome back to Denmark.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I humbly thank you, sir. Dost know this water-fly?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: No, my good lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thy state is the more gracious; for ’tis a vice to know

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: him. He hath much land, and fertile: let a beast be lord

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of beasts, and his crib shall stand at the king’s mess: ’tis

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a chough; but, as I say, spacious in the possession of dirt.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Sweet lord, if your lordship were at leisure, I should impart a

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: thing to you from his majesty.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will receive it, sir, with all diligence of spirit. Put your

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: bonnet to his right use; ’tis for the head.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: I thank your lordship, it is very hot.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, believe me, ’tis very cold; the wind is northerly.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: It is indifferent cold, my lord, indeed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But yet methinks it is very sultry and hot for my complexion.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Exceedingly, my lord; it is very sultry—as ’twere—I cannot tell how. But,

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: my lord, his majesty bade me signify to you that he has

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: laid a great wager on your head: sir, this is the matter—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I beseech you, remember—Hamlet moves him to put on his hat.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Nay, good my lord; for mine ease, in good faith. Sir, here

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: is newly come to court Laertes; believe me, an absolute gentleman, full

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: of most excellent differences, of very soft society and great showing: indeed,

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: to speak feelingly of him, he is the card or calendar of

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: gentry, for you shall find in him the continent of what part

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: a gentleman would see.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, his definement suffers no perdition in you; though, I know, to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: divide him inventorially would dizzy the arithmetic of memory, and yet but

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: yaw neither, in respect of his quick sail. But, in the verity

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of extolment, I take him to be a soul of great article;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and his infusion of such dearth and rareness, as, to make true

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: diction of him, his semblable is his mirror; and who else would

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: trace him, his umbrage, nothing more.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Your lordship speaks most infallibly of him.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The concernancy, sir? why do we wrap the gentleman in our more rawer breath?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Sir?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Is’t not possible to understand in another tongue? You will do’t, sir, really.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What imports the nomination of this gentleman?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Of Laertes?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: His purse is empty already; all’s golden words are spent.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Of him, sir.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: I know you are not ignorant—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I would you did, sir; yet, in faith, if you did, it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: would not much approve me. Well, sir?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: You are not ignorant of what excellence Laertes is—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I dare not confess that, lest I should compare with him in

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: excellence; but, to know a man well, were to know himself.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: I mean, sir, for his weapon; but in the imputation laid on

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: him by them, in his meed he’s unfellowed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What’s his weapon?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Rapier and dagger.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That’s two of his weapons: but, well.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: The king, sir, hath wagered with him six Barbary horses: against the

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: which he has imponed, as I take it, six French rapiers and

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: poniards, with their assigns, as girdle, hangers, and so: three of the

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: carriages, in faith, are very dear to fancy, very responsive to the

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: hilts, most delicate carriages, and of very liberal conceit.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What call you the carriages?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I knew you must be edified by the margent ere you had done.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: The carriages, sir, are the hangers.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The phrase would be more german to the matter, if we could

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: carry cannon by our sides: I would it might be hangers till

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: then. But, on: six Barbary horses against six French swords, their assigns,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and three liberal-conceited carriages; that’s the French bet against the Danish. Why

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: is this “imponed,” as you call it?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: The king, sir, hath laid, that in a dozen passes between yourself

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: and him, he shall not exceed you three hits: he hath laid

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: on twelve for nine; and it would come to immediate trial, if

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: your lordship would vouchsafe the answer.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How if I answer “no”?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: I mean, my lord, the opposition of your person in trial.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, I will walk here in the hall: if it please his

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: majesty, ’tis the breathing time of day with me; let the foils

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: be brought, the gentleman willing, and the king hold his purpose, I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: will win for him an I can; if not, I will gain

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: nothing but my shame and the odd hits.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Shall I re-deliver you e’en so?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To this effect, sir; after what flourish your nature will.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: I commend my duty to your lordship.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Yours, yours. He does well to commend it himself; there are no

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: tongues else for’s turn.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: This lapwing runs away with the shell on his head.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He did comply with his dug, before he sucked it. Thus has

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: he—and many more of the same bevy that I know the drossy

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: age dotes on—only got the tune of the time and outward habit

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of encounter; a kind of yesty collection, which carries them through and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: through the most fond and winnowed opinions; and do but blow them

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: to their trial, the bubbles are out.

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: My lord, his majesty commended him to you by young Osric, who

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: brings back to him that you attend him in the hall: he

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: sends to know if your pleasure hold to play with Laertes, or

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: that you will take longer time.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am constant to my purpose; they follow the king’s pleasure: if

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: his fitness speaks, mine is ready; now or whensoever, provided I be

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: so able as now.

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: The king and queen and all are coming down.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In happy time.

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: The queen desires you to use some gentle entertainment to Laertes before

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: you fall to play.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: She well instructs me.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: You will lose this wager, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I do not think so: since he went into France, I have

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: been in continual practice: I shall win at the odds. But thou

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: wouldst not think how ill all’s here about my heart: but it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: is no matter.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Nay, good my lord—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It is but foolery; but it is such a kind of gain-giving,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: as would perhaps trouble a woman.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: If your mind dislike any thing, obey it: I will forestall their

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: repair hither, and say you are not fit.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Not a whit, we defy augury: there’s a special providence in the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: fall of a sparrow. If it be now, ’tis not to come;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: if it be not to come, it will be now; if it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: be not now, yet it will come: the readiness is all: since

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: no man has aught of what he leaves, what is’t to leave

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: betimes?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Come, Hamlet, come, and take this hand from me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Give me your pardon, sir: I’ve done you wrong;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But pardon’t, as you are a gentleman.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This presence knows,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And you must needs have heard, how I am punish’d

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With sore distraction. What I have done,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That might your nature, honour and exception

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Roughly awake, I here proclaim was madness.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Was’t Hamlet wrong’d Laertes? Never Hamlet:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If Hamlet from himself be ta’en away,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And when he’s not himself does wrong Laertes,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then Hamlet does it not, Hamlet denies it.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Who does it, then? His madness: if’t be so,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hamlet is of the faction that is wrong’d;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: His madness is poor Hamlet’s enemy.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, in this audience,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let my disclaiming from a purposed evil

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Free me so far in your most generous thoughts,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That I have shot mine arrow o’er the house,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And hurt my brother.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I am satisfied in nature,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Whose motive, in this case, should stir me most

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To my revenge: but in my terms of honour

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I stand aloof; and will no reconcilement,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Till by some elder masters, of known honour,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I have a voice and precedent of peace,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To keep my name ungored. But till that time,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I do receive your offer’d love like love,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And will not wrong it.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I embrace it freely;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And will this brother’s wager frankly play.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Give us the foils. Come on.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Come, one for me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll be your foil, Laertes: in mine ignorance

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Your skill shall, like a star i’ the darkest night,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Stick fiery off indeed.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: You mock me, sir.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, by this hand.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Give them the foils, young Osric. Cousin Hamlet,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You know the wager?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Very well, my lord;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Your grace hath laid the odds o’ the weaker side.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I do not fear it; I have seen you both:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But since he is better’d, we have therefore odds.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: This is too heavy, let me see another.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This likes me well. These foils have all a length? They prepare to play.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Ay, my good lord.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Set me the stoups of wine upon that table.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: If Hamlet give the first or second hit,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Or quit in answer of the third exchange,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Let all the battlements their ordnance fire;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The king shall drink to Hamlet’s better breath;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And in the cup an union shall he throw,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Richer than that which four successive kings

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In Denmark’s crown have worn. Give me the cups;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And let the kettle to the trumpet speak,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The trumpet to the cannoneer without,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The cannons to the heavens, the heavens to earth,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: “Now the king drinks to Hamlet.” Come, begin:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And you, the judges, bear a wary eye.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Come on, sir.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Come, my lord. They play.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: One.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Judgment.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: A hit, a very palpable hit.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Well; again.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Stay; give me drink. Hamlet, this pearl is thine;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Here’s to thy health. Give him the cup.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll play this bout first; set it by awhile. Come. They play.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Another hit; what say you?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: A touch, a touch, I do confess.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our son shall win.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: He’s fat, and scant of breath.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Here, Hamlet, take my napkin, rub thy brows:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: The queen carouses to thy fortune, Hamlet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Good madam!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Gertrude, do not drink.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I will, my lord; I pray you, pardon me.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It is the poison’d cup: it is too late.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I dare not drink yet, madam; by and by.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Come, let me wipe thy face.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: My lord, I’ll hit him now.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I do not think’t.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And yet ’tis almost ’gainst my conscience.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Come, for the third, Laertes: you but dally;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I pray you, pass with your best violence;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Say you so? come on. They play.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Nothing, neither way.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Have at you now! Laertes wounds Hamlet; then, in scuffling, they change

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: rapiers, and Hamlet wounds Laertes.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Part them; they are incensed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nay, come, again. The Queen falls.

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Look to the queen there, ho!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: They bleed on both sides. How is it, my lord?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: How is’t, Laertes?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Why, as a woodcock to mine own springe, Osric;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I am justly kill’d with mine own treachery.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How does the queen?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: She swounds to see them bleed.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: No, no, the drink, the drink—O my dear Hamlet—

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: The drink, the drink! I am poison’d.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O villany! Ho! let the door be lock’d:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Treachery! Seek it out.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: It is here, Hamlet: Hamlet, thou art slain;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: No medicine in the world can do thee good;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: In thee there is not half an hour of life;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The treacherous instrument is in thy hand,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Unbated and envenom’d: the foul practice

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Hath turn’d itself on me; lo, here I lie,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Never to rise again: thy mother’s poison’d:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I can no more: the king, the king’s to blame.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The point envenom’d too!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then, venom, to thy work.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Treason! treason!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O, yet defend me, friends; I am but hurt.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Here, thou incestuous, murderous, damned Dane,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Drink off this potion. Is thy union here?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Follow my mother. King dies.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: He is justly served;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: It is a poison temper’d by himself.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Exchange forgiveness with me, noble Hamlet:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Mine and my father’s death come not upon thee,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Nor thine on me!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Heaven make thee free of it! I follow thee.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am dead, Horatio. Wretched queen, adieu!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You that look pale and tremble at this chance,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That are but mutes or audience to this act,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Had I but time—as this fell sergeant, death,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Is strict in his arrest—O, I could tell you—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But let it be. Horatio, I am dead;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thou livest; report me and my cause aright

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To the unsatisfied.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Never believe it:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I am more an antique Roman than a Dane:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Here’s yet some liquor left.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As thou’rt a man,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Give me the cup: let go; by heaven, I’ll have’t.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O good Horatio, what a wounded name,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Things standing thus unknown, shall live behind me!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If thou didst ever hold me in thy heart,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Absent thee from felicity awhile,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And in this harsh world draw thy breath in pain,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To tell my story. March afar off, and shot within. What warlike noise is this?

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: Young Fortinbras, with conquest come from Poland,

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: To the ambassadors of England gives

lines.speaker: OSRIC
lines.text: This warlike volley.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, I die, Horatio;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The potent poison quite o’er-crows my spirit:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I cannot live to hear the news from England;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But I do prophesy the election lights

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: On Fortinbras: he has my dying voice;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So tell him, with the occurrents, more and less,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Which have solicited. The rest is silence.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Now cracks a noble heart. Good night, sweet prince;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And flights of angels sing thee to thy rest!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Why does the drum come hither? March within.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Where is this sight?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What is it ye would see?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: If aught of woe or wonder, cease your search.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: This quarry cries on havoc. O proud death,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: What feast is toward in thine eternal cell,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: That thou so many princes at a shot

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: So bloodily hast struck?

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: The sight is dismal;

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: And our affairs from England come too late:

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: The ears are senseless that should give us hearing,

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: To tell him his commandment is fulfill’d,

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: That Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead:

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Where should we have our thanks?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Not from his mouth,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Had it the ability of life to thank you:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: He never gave commandment for their death.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But since, so jump upon this bloody question,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: You from the Polack wars, and you from England,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Are here arrived give order that these bodies

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: High on a stage be placed to the view;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And let me speak to the yet unknowing world

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: How these things came about: so shall you hear

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of carnal, bloody, and unnatural acts,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of accidental judgments, casual slaughters,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of deaths put on by cunning and forced cause,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And, in this upshot, purposes mistook

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Fall’n on the inventors’ heads: all this can I

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Truly deliver.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Let us haste to hear it,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: And call the noblest to the audience.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: For me, with sorrow I embrace my fortune:

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: I have some rights of memory in this kingdom,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Which now to claim my vantage doth invite me.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of that I shall have also cause to speak,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And from his mouth whose voice will draw on more:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But let this same be presently perform’d,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Even while men’s minds are wild; lest more mischance,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: On plots and errors, happen.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Let four captains

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Bear Hamlet, like a soldier, to the stage;

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: For he was likely, had he been put on,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: To have proved most royally: and, for his passage,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: The soldiers’ music and the rites of war

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Speak loudly for him.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Take up the bodies: such a sight as this

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Becomes the field, but here shows much amiss.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Go, bid the soldiers shoot. A dead march. Exeunt, bearing off the dead bodies; after which a peal of ordnance is shot off.

stage_directions.text: Enter King, Queen, Laertes, Lords, Osric, and Attendants with foils, etc.
The King puts Laertes’ hand into Hamlet’s.
Trumpets sound, and cannon shot off within.
Enter Fortinbras, the English Ambassadors, and others.

glosses.definition: however we crudely shape events, a divinity perfects them

glosses.phrase: stay the grinding of the axe
glosses.definition: wait for the axe to be sharpened
thrust himself into the royal succession

glosses.phrase: to quit him with this arm
glosses.definition: to repay him with this hand
the time between will be mine to act

annotations.title: Providence and the altered commission
annotations.body: Hamlet's narrative of the voyage — 'Rashly, / And praised be rashness for it' — articulates the play's only positive theology. 'There's a divinity that shapes our ends, / Rough-hew them how we will' is a claim the earlier Hamlet could not have made. The man who spent three acts immobilized by questions of action now ascribes the sealed commission's discovery to providence rather than to his own cleverness. The theological turn is easy to sentimentalize; Rhodri Lewis has argued that the play's providence-talk is not consolation but the calm that comes with surrendering agency. Hamlet is not saved; he is spared long enough to complete the revenge that will destroy him. The speech's peace is the peace of a man who has stopped trying to master his fate.
sources: Rhodri Lewis, Hamlet and the Vision of Darkness (2017)

annotations.title: 'The readiness is all'
annotations.body: Hamlet's response to Horatio's warning about the duel — 'We defy augury. There's a special providence in the fall of a sparrow... The readiness is all' — echoes Matthew 10:29 ('one sparrow shall not fall on the ground without your Father') and Edgar's 'ripeness is all' in King Lear. What the prince arrives at, after five acts of deliberation, is not a philosophy of action but a philosophy of availability. One cannot control when one is called; one can only be ready when called. The speech is Hamlet's final utterance before the catastrophe, and its stoic-Christian fusion is where the play's religious and philosophical traditions quiet into a single voice.
sources: A. D. Nuttall, Shakespeare the Thinker (2007)

annotations.title: The choreography of death
annotations.body: The final fencing match is a tragic mechanism: one instrument of death, two accidental redirections, four bodies. Laertes is wounded with the poisoned foil he meant for Hamlet; Gertrude drinks from the cup intended for Hamlet; Hamlet, learning the truth, poisons Claudius with his own potion. Each death is an inversion of the killer's intent. Shakespeare's revenge-tragedy precursors, particularly Kyd's Spanish Tragedy, tended toward operatic pile-ups of corpses; Hamlet's ending is more economical, each death following from a specific choice. The King is killed last, and by his own contrivance; the revenge Hamlet never managed to design, Claudius has done for him.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: 'The rest is silence'
annotations.body: Hamlet's last words are simultaneously a statement about what cannot be said and an acknowledgment of what will be said after him — Fortinbras will inherit, Horatio will tell the story, the theater of Denmark will continue. The Folio has four additional syllables ('O, o, o, o') that some editors print and others suppress. If performed, these dying groans convert the neat aphorism into something more ragged. T. S. Eliot famously called 'the rest is silence' the cleanest ending in Shakespeare; others, including Bradley, found in the 'O, o, o, o' a more honest human ending. Either way, the phrase is Shakespeare's most condensed meditation on the limits of speech at the edge of death.
sources: T. S. Eliot, 'Hamlet and His Problems' (1919)

annotations.title: Fortinbras's arrival and the restoration
annotations.body: Fortinbras enters as the play closes, returning from the Polish campaign and stepping almost by chance into the throne of Denmark. His claim — 'some rights of memory in this kingdom' — recalls his father's lost lands. The restoration he provides is not consoling. The young man who organized an army to 'find quarrel in a straw' inherits a kingdom emptied by a more meditative son. Shakespeare's political vision is bleak: the state that has consumed itself through corruption is passed to an outsider whose decisiveness is untouched by the questions Hamlet could not stop asking. The final 'soldier's music' given to Hamlet honors the prince who never quite got to be a warrior.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet discover when he unseals the commission on the ship?
options: Claudius's confession of the murder.
An order to the King of England to execute Hamlet on arrival.
A ransom demand for his release.
A letter of abdication in Hamlet's favor.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet mean by 'There's a divinity that shapes our ends, rough-hew them how we will'?
options: That Hamlet has become a carpenter.
That a providential design operates beyond human attempts to shape events.
That fate is entirely indifferent to human action.
That only kings can shape history.

trials.prompt: What biblical passage does 'There's a special providence in the fall of a sparrow' echo?
options: The Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5).
Jesus's instruction to the apostles — Matthew 10:29 ('one sparrow shall not fall on the ground without your Father').
Psalm 23 ('The Lord is my shepherd').
The book of Job.
Laertes stabs her with the poisoned foil.
She drinks from the poisoned cup Claudius intended for Hamlet.
She collapses from grief.
Hamlet poisons her to punish her remarriage.

trials.prompt: How does Hamlet finally kill Claudius?
options: He stabs him with the poisoned foil and forces him to drink the poisoned cup.
He strangles him with his own crown's circlet.
He has Horatio run him through with a sword.
He drowns him in a font.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet ask Horatio to do after his death?
options: Flee Denmark immediately.
Live on to tell Hamlet's story and report the truth aright.
Poison himself to accompany Hamlet.
Destroy the letter that proved Claudius's guilt.

trials.prompt: What is the significance of Hamlet's final words, 'The rest is silence'?
options: A literal request for quiet in the room.
An acknowledgment that what remains — of his story, of the afterlife, of himself — cannot be spoken.
A reference to a musical score.
A coded instruction to Horatio about a hidden document.
